results.possibleIdentity: Pedro Lujan (born 1943) is a Chicano sculptor and artist originally from El Paso, Texas, who became a prominent figure in the New York art scene starting in the mid-1960s. He earned a Bachelor of Arts from the University of Texas at El Paso in 1965 and later a Master of Arts from Goddard College in 1973. Lujan is recognized for his involvement with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), notably participating in an E.A.T. exhibition between November 1968 and February 1969 in collaboration with E. C. Hyde. He also played a technical and creative role in the early video art movement as a close associate of the Videofreex collective; he supervised the construction of the "Blue Calzone," a massive vinyl inflatable rear-projection structure used during the Alternate Media Conference at Goddard College in 1970. Throughout the 1970s and 1980s, Lujan received significant recognition for his sculpture work, including a Guggenheim Fellowship and multiple grants from the National Endowment for the Arts (1974, 1977). His work has been featured in exhibitions at El Museo del Barrio, the Just Above Midtown (JAM) gallery, and the Roswell Artist-in-Residence program.

results.possibleIdentity: Howard Gutstadt is a pioneering video artist and media activist who was highly active in the late 1960s and 1970s. He was a prominent member of early video collectives in New York City, including the People's Video Theater (PVT) and Survival Arts Media (SAM), which he co-founded in 1972. Gutstadt's work often utilized early portable video technology, such as the Sony Portapak, to document counterculture movements, facilitate community storytelling, and explore video as a tool for social and political change. His groups were contemporary with and occasionally shared program space with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) during the early years of public access cable television in New York. Gutstadt was also a member of 'Commediation,' a pioneering video discussion group that included Nam June Paik. In the late 1970s, he relocated to California, where he shifted focus toward the development of digital imaging software and tools for artists, working with companies like Sony, Autodesk, and Diaquest. His archival materials are currently held in collections such as the NYU Special Collections Library and the BAVC Media archive.
